author: @pharmakomantis
text:
There are no watts in the wind.
Wind is not electricity – it is motion in the medium: air.
What you’re doing when generating electricity is simply rotating a coil so that it intersects the dielectric and magnetic field lines of a magnet. That’s where the response comes from.

Electricity isn’t pulled from the wind.
It emerges from the interaction between motion and field.
As Charles Proteus Steinmetz said: Electricity is the interaction of the dielectric and magnetic. It is not a thing in itself.

This is why geometry matters. If the copper doesn’t have the right angle, you won’t properly cut through the field — and you’ll get nothing.
What we have are two conjugate fields, and if you intersect them correctly through motion, the result is electricity: a hybrid of dielectric and magnetic.

For those who struggle with this type of language:
Magnetic = what we call current (amperes)
Dielectric = what we call voltage (potential)

❝There is no such thing as “electricity” as a substance. Electricity is not energy.
It is a combination of electrostatic (dielectric) and electromagnetic (magnetic) phenomena, resulting from a disturbance in the ether.❞
— Charles P. Steinmetz, ca. 1916, Union College

⸻

But what does it really mean to “cut through” the field?

A magnet is not “a source of magnetic energy.”
A magnet is the spatial geometry of two conjugate fields in opposition.
The dielectric is radial, inward/outward — a field of potential.
The magnetic is toroidal — a field of motion.
These two are not separate — they are counterspaces of each other.

You don’t extract energy from a magnet.
You disturb the resting balance of dielectric–magnetic conjugation.
Electricity is the result of motion across this balance.

❝Electricity is not made of particles. It is a field phenomenon.❞
— Nikola Tesla, ca. 1891, Electrical Engineers

❝Every particle is a wave. Every wave is a field. Every field is a motion through the ether.❞
— Tesla Papers, Colorado Springs Notes, 1899

❝We are whirling through endless space with an inconceivable speed. Everything is spinning. The source of all motion is the ether.
We only tap it when we move through it in the right way.❞
— Nikola Tesla, New York Herald Tribune, 1931

---------------------------- 

Love your videos =) ❤

author: @BenEehayeh
text:
Instead of an outer sleave you should have another cylinder of magnets.
Gauss rating is at the surface of the magnet. The further away the copper from the surface of the magnet the greater loss of gauss.
To combat this you need to force the field lines of the magnets to travel to another magnet. This will improve efficiency by creating a magnetic circuit.
Double the magnets for two rotors and one stator.

replies.text: Voltage = EMF x length of wire x relative speed between wire and EMF... Yes, doubling the length of the wire and magnets will double the voltage

author: @Ewr42
text:
Have you tried using halbach arrangements instead of NSNSNS?

Isn't the best magnetic field the one with the most cross section going through the wires perpendiculary?

NSNSNSNS looks like small circles, the opposite of the serpentine coil's objective

How to not waste the part of the field that is perpendicular to the surface of the magnets? And the part of the field that's pointlessly in the middle of the rotor?

Maybe it's only relevant in bigger rotors so that the geometry of the rotor is closer to being flat(larger diameter), or not at all

But you've optimized for the best electric field reaction to the accelerated magnetic field, but what about optimizing the magnetic field itself?

I'd love to see a 2d halbach array using many small magnets, maybe offset/intercalated so that the resulting magnetic field is like a larger diameter cylinder parallel to the surface of the magnets/rotor.

I really hope there's something useful around this idea

author: @bmobert
text:
You should be able to increase the useful Flux and the power density with a halbach array built using two levels. If the magnets have magnetic fields oriented along the shortest length --assuming the same magnet shape-- then you could use the 3d-prints as--is, alternating North, south radially; if you then modify the magnet holder (is that the armeture?) so that a second ring of magnets are inboard of the exterior ring and can be oriented to make a halbach array. (IE, every north of the inner ring is oriented annularly towards a south of the radially oriented outer ring.) Thus, with only a minor modification, this design should be able to double or triple the usable magnetic Flux: twice the magnets with a field shaped into the used volume.

The magnets are in the center, right? I'm suddenly forgetting the assembly pattern. Regardless, the above comments assume the magnets are internal of the coils. The described geometry would have to change (though only slightly: the radial and annular componets swap) if the coils were within the magnet housing.

author: @geraldelwood9660
text: More stimulating information. But I have to point out one tiny error - The serpentine configuration does not give a higher ration of copper wires in the vertical plane to the horizontal plane (tops/bottoms) over a standard coil configuration. But that takes nothing away from the other advantages of the serpentine configuration.

author: @dmcginnis427
text: I love your channel! I am not sure it has been mentioned before you could get more flux through the coil if you returned the magnetic flux on the rotor.  You have a high reluctance path from the back of the magnets to the front of the magnets so you lose half the magnetic field that comes from the back of the magnets. You could build a low reluctance path to return the magnetic field from the magnet face that faces the coils to the back of the magnet by building the  rotor in the shape of a cup made of magnetic material. The stator would fit inside the cup. The rotor cup would spin around the stator. You need the rotor in a cup shape so you don't make eddy currents - makes things a bit heavier and harder to build but you should be able to double the flux, hence the efficiency. Carry on!

author: @steveclayton5692
text: ​Robert you have inspired me to make ​these coils, though I have taken it a step further . I own punch presses and do tool and die work. I have built tooling which will form right angle serpentine coils out of 1/4" or 3/8" solid thick copper. No wrapping of single strands. The die is a progressive die set and the serpentine is taken up on a larger computerized machine coil, normally used for metal stamping of parts. Can cut to length making the diameter nearly any size wanted. Willing to supply samples for anyone interested. Keep up the good work.

author: @leostarling5746
text: I suggest (again) inserting a steel cylinder inside the rotor. This will magnetically connect the N/S back ends of the magnets, strengthening the magnetic field intersecting your serpentine coil. You will have to apply more torque but will get higher voltage out, which makes the generator even more effective for its size.

replies.author: @stoffel89
replies.text: Will it make a difference if the steel cylinder touches the magnets? or should the magnets and steel be separated? If it should be separated, does the distance from the magnets to the steel have an effect on the efficiency of the generator?

replies.author: @leostarling5746
replies.text: @stoffel89 yes, distance will weaken the magnetic field

replies.author: @Léon-XT
replies.text: Or place the magnets in a Halbach Array setup, even better than a steel cylinder 😉

replies.author: @georgeblack589
replies.text: It's magnet wire, the insulation is a lacquer over the entire surface of the wire. It's the same product used in electric motors and transformers. If a vinyl insulation was used, the coils would be massive and massively inefficient.

author: @blackredroll
text: I've designed and 3D printed a few iterations of this type generator, but instead preparing serpentine coils separately, I winded wire around rotor to create 3 serpentines. I used  0.5 mm wire and 20x10x10 neodymium magnets and I got disappointed. It was giving around 2.5W and barely reaching 5V at 500 rpm. Changing wire to 0.25 mm would increase voltage, but power would stay the same (increased resistance, lower current). If I were to get 5V to e.g. charge USB devices, which is better setup, low voltage generator with step-up converter or higher V generator with step-down converter?

author: @travismoore7849
text: I made a capacitor that was a cross between a laden jar and an electrolytic capacitor by suspending it in air with a bit of dental floss. The graphite electrodes and wax insulator with a thin paper cloth soaked in salt water made the capacitor. I charged it up in the microwave, and it had a very high voltage charge when I stopped zapping it. Though, it seemed like the electric field of the capacitor contained the plasma of the arcing carbon electrodes. It looked like a sun in a jar as to how bright it was. I wonder if fusion reactions could be made and contained in such a device using hydrogen boron 11 gas.

author: @PeterGMerchant
text: Before I watched this video I had an idea about how you could fit this sort of thing in a downpipe. The diameter looks about the same, so the idea is to have the serpentine coil just inside the downpipe and a hollow rotor vertically mounted with the secret ingredient of a fan or archimedes screw inside the rotor so that rainwater turns it to generate  electricity. OK, some losses due to structure to hold the axle in place, and probably a conical shape to cover the coils  and direct water to the centre. Consider that water is denser than air. Well, something to think about.

author: @bretthorwood9396

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard

replies.author: @georgeblack589
replies.text: There is still only so much power available. Like he said, if there's 5 watts in the wind, you can't get more than 5 watts out. Sure, you may increase the voltage with a transformer, but then your amperage is abysmally low. The inverse is true as well, you could get higher amperage...at 1 volt.

author: @SwiftNuts
text: I'm actually working on an incredibly similar mechanism myself, glad to see a three phase serpentine coil works just fine👍

replies.author: @Unpopular_0pinion
replies.text: Hey what's the benefit of three phase? Would you mind telling me? Trying to learn

replies.author: @SwiftNuts
replies.text: @Unpopular_0pinion well just being able to overlap the coils and fit more copper into the stator is one major advantage in the serpentine's case, then there's the point that three-phase generators can be very easily converted into motors with a controller, and finally I'd say a three phase generator if wired correctly should be able to produce a much higher frequency of ac current with the same speed of rotation.

replies.author: @Unpopular_0pinion
replies.text: @SwiftNuts Oh okay🙂 And what are the benefits of higher frequency AC? Does that mean more power?

replies.author: @SwiftNuts
replies.text: @Unpopular_0pinion not generally it's just able to be used in a wider variety of situations without needing extra electronic components. Being able to take up the empty space with more windings does allow for more power out of certain configurations however.

author: @Howtosolveproblems
text: I'm puzzled. Excuse me if this question is dumb. That serpentine coil seems to have 6 'ups', 6 'downs', 6 'tops' and 6 'bottoms'. Perhaps my error is in assuming that you can't count each vertical twice when comparing the potential power output of the serpentine to its equivalent made from separate complete loops, so I assume that this serpentine is equivalent to 6 separate loops? If so, 6 separate coils would also exhibit 6 'ups', 6 'downs', 6 'tops' and 6 'bottoms'.  So, in the serpentine equivalent how has wire been saved?

replies.author: @stevengouws8684
replies.text: 6 ups + 6 downs =12 coils. 1 top + 1 bottom = 1 coil. 6 tops + 6 bottoms = 6 coils. So 12 coils but only 6 coils worth of tops and bottoms. Not to mention the time and energy spent making 12 separate coils Verse 1 easy to make serpentine coil  :)

replies.author: @Howtosolveproblems
replies.text: @stevengouws8684 Hi Steven. Thanks for the reply. Unfortunately, every time I try to continue the discussion my comment is deleted.

replies.author: @Howtosolveproblems
replies.text: @stevengouws8684 Hi there. Okay, so I assume the comment deletions are as a result of links, or mention of Inst......m so we must resort to text. I will persist, as this is driving me a bit mad. To identify 12 coils in the serpentine array, do we not have to count each up and down in adjacent coils twice? I can't quite see how I can count 12 without doing that. Is that valid? Does each vertical bunch of wire generate twice the power of its coil equivalent? If so, how? What am I missing? Thanks for the help.

replies.author: @stevengouws8684
replies.text: @Howtosolveproblems Not sure each vertical coil generates 2x the normal coil. It generates at it's most effective for copper used, and angle of magnetic field passing through the wire (90'). It's far easier to make, uses less copper and is more effective. Using different magnets and different copper wire gauges, will yield different results, as well as RPM of the magnetic stator. However focusing purely on the coils, if you used as much copper of the same gauge to make a serpentine coil, as opposed to standard (12) SEPERATE COILS, you will ( should ) get a better result . You have to test and see for yourself, nothing like it.  :)

replies.author: @Howtosolveproblems
replies.text: @stevengouws8684 Oh, I quite agree. I don't quite understand how each vertical can be providing twice the power. Hence, when I add up 6 ups and 6 downs I count 6 coils as I don't think I can count these verticals twice to count 12. It's not my topic though, so I assume I'm making an error somewhere, I just don't know where. The reason I'm trying to work this out is that I have indeed tried this for myself and still don't see the advantage the serpentine offers. I've built a rudimentary wind turbine on the roof of my shed and I've just about completed a generator which I'm documenting online. I wanted to offer a diagram of how I'm counting this and invite some opinions, but this seems to be completely forbidden in YouTube comments. Even mention of an external resource seems to cause automated conniptions, which is rather inconvenient when one is trying to have a discussion.

author: @Berkana
text:
Could you combine a Halbach array with a serpentine coil? How would you do that to best effect?

Halbach arrays use the magnetic field most efficiently by pushing all of the magnetic field to one side of the array.

replies.author: @stevecollins4567
replies.text: Please say more

replies.author: @pottervi
replies.text: If you put a magnet in-between the north/south magnets but turn it 90 degrees so the same polarity facing the  magnet next to it, it forces the flux to go out farther and stronger. It makes it act like a stronger magnet

replies.author: @pottervi
replies.text:
Hmm that looks hard to follow. Let me see if I can find a link

Can't find it but it's basically a halboch array going around the rotor

replies.author: @Berkana
@matslarsson8790
replies.text: The strong side of a halbach array is a mix of north and south fields, and will not work. It must be north OR south. But one way to increase the strength of the magnets on the rotor is to use an iron core in the middle instead of just plastics, then they will amplify themselves just like when stacking magnets on each other.

author: @dexterdixon2000
text: I’m not sure there’s actually much copper saving here mate… if you do a  set of standard coils and link them in series with single connection cables (as per a standard series star wound machine) you have the same number of radial bunches of wire as tangential bunches… which is what the serpentine has… maybe I’m missing something but are you able to clear that up for me?

replies.author: @Howtosolveproblems
replies.text: Hi there. I've just posted a similar observation, but am no electrician so am very much scratching my head. I can't quite see how wire is saved.

replies.author: @dexterdixon2000
replies.text: @murdocks4304 ithanks - i understand its only the sides. I still dont think theres any less copper - in a standard coil setup (using rectangular coils) you have two sideways runs for every two uprights then you link the coils with single pieces of wire. With the serpentine you have the same - two sideways for every two uprights?

author: @EUPThatsMe
text:
My first attempt with a 32 magnet rotor and I managed to get one of them in backwards - now it produces at best ~0.8VAC (100 turns of 26ga). 
I'm not sure how to get the one magnet out of its recessed hole - I might have to shatter it - can you drill Neodymium?
After that is a new coil of 300+ turns of 41ga wire that just came in.

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard
replies.text: Sad to report that although TECHNICALLY you can drill it, not really; it will likely shatter and even if it breaks cleanly you may find it has demagnetized as I did. I wonder if slowly grinding it might work if you keep the heat down. But that's a huge pain too and I don't know for sure if it works. Easier just to smash the magnet imo.

replies.author: @EUPThatsMe
replies.text: @TheBaconWizard My main concern is getting it out cleanly enough to replace it.

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard
replies.text: @EUPThatsMe What is the rotor made-of and what glue, if any, did you use?

replies.author: @EUPThatsMe
replies.text: @TheBaconWizard Its PLA with CA+Baking soda glue. While Acetone would not do much to the PLA I don't want to risk loosening the others nearby.

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard
replies.text: @EUPThatsMe Eh, acetone does soften and weaknen PLA. I think your best bet might be to cut the whole thing and glue a new section in. Then balance it incase you've made it heavier or lighter on one side. That would be my approach I think.
